title: Severe bleeding
steps: Make sure the scene is safe and use gloves or a clean barrier if available.
Press firmly and continuously on the wound with gauze or the cleanest cloth available.
If blood soaks through, add more material on top. Do not remove the first layer.
Keep pressure on the wound. If trained and bleeding from an arm or leg will not stop, use a commercial tourniquet as directed.
Keep the person still and warm. Seek trained medical help as soon as any route is available.
avoid: Do not probe the wound or remove a deeply embedded object.
Do not release pressure repeatedly to check whether bleeding has stopped.
source_name: American Red Cross - First Aid Steps

title: Unsafe drinking water
steps: Use sealed bottled water if it is available and undamaged.
For cloudy water, let sediment settle and pour off the clearer water, or filter it through a clean cloth.
Bring clear water to a rolling boil for 1 minute. Above 6,500 feet (about 2,000 meters), boil for 3 minutes.
Let it cool naturally and store it in a clean, tightly covered container.
Use safe water for drinking, cooking, brushing teeth, washing dishes, and making infant formula.
avoid: Boiling or disinfecting cannot make water contaminated by fuel, toxic chemicals, or radioactive material safe.
Do not drink water from home heating radiators or boilers.
source_name: CDC - How to Make Water Safe in an Emergency

title: Fire in a building
steps: Leave immediately by the nearest safe exit. Warn others as you go.
Stay low under smoke and crawl if necessary.
Feel a closed door with the back of your hand before opening it. Use another route if it is hot.
Close doors behind you to slow fire and smoke.
Once outside, move to the meeting point and do not re-enter.
avoid: Do not use an elevator.
Do not stop to collect belongings or return for people, pets, or equipment.

title: Unresponsive and not breathing normally
steps: Check the scene, then shout and tap the person. Check breathing for no more than 10 seconds.
Tell someone to contact emergency services and bring an AED if any service or device is reachable.
Place the person on a firm, flat surface.
Start chest compressions in the center of the chest: hard and fast, 100 to 120 per minute.
Use an AED as soon as available and follow its prompts. Continue until normal breathing returns or trained help takes over.
avoid: Gasping is not normal breathing.
Do not delay compressions while searching for equipment.

title: Flash flood
steps: Move immediately to higher ground or the highest safe level of a sturdy building.
Follow marked evacuation routes if they remain open.
If trapped in a building, move upward but avoid a closed attic unless you can reach the roof.
If trapped in a vehicle in rapidly moving water, leave for higher ground only when doing so is safer than remaining inside.
Listen to a battery radio or local alerts when available.
avoid: Never walk, swim, or drive through floodwater.
Stay away from bridges over fast-moving water and from fallen power lines.

title: Possible stroke
steps: Use FAST: check for Face drooping, Arm weakness, and Speech difficulty.
Note the exact time symptoms started or the last time the person was known to be well.
Contact emergency medical help by any available route. Stroke treatment is time-sensitive.
Keep the person resting in a safe position and monitor breathing.
If the person becomes unresponsive and is not breathing normally, start CPR.
avoid: Do not give food, drink, aspirin, or other medicine unless a clinician directs it.
Do not let the person drive.
source_name: CDC - Signs and Symptoms of Stroke

title: Heatstroke
steps: Move the person to shade or a cooler place and remove excess clothing.
Contact emergency medical help by any available route.
Cool rapidly with cool water, wet cloths, a fan, or ice packs at the neck, armpits, and groin.
If safe and practical, immerse the body in cool water while keeping the airway clear.
Monitor breathing and be ready to start CPR.
avoid: Do not give fluids to a confused, vomiting, seizing, or unconscious person.
Do not use fever medicine; heatstroke is not an infection-related fever.

title: Hypothermia
steps: Move the person out of wind and wet conditions into shelter.
Remove wet clothing gently and replace it with dry layers and blankets.
Warm the center of the body first: chest, neck, head, and groin.
Give warm, sweet, nonalcoholic drinks only if the person is fully awake and can swallow.
Handle gently, monitor breathing, and seek trained help.
avoid: Do not use direct intense heat, hot water, heating lamps, or rub the arms and legs.
Do not give alcohol.

title: Snakebite
steps: Move away from the snake. Do not try to capture or kill it.
Keep the person calm, still, and lying or sitting with the bite in a neutral comfortable position.
Remove rings, watches, and tight clothing before swelling begins.
Wash loosely with soap and water if available, then cover with a clean dry dressing.
Mark the edge of swelling with the time and seek emergency medical care urgently.
avoid: Do not cut or suck the wound, apply ice, use electric shock, or apply a tourniquet.
Do not give alcohol or pain medicines that increase bleeding unless directed by a clinician.

title: Extended power outage
steps: Use flashlights instead of candles where possible.
Keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed. A refrigerator stays cold about 4 hours when unopened.
Disconnect sensitive electronics to reduce surge damage when power returns.
Use generators outdoors at least 20 feet from windows, doors, and vents.
Prioritize safe water, medications, temperature protection, and battery radio updates.
avoid: Never use a generator, grill, or fuel-burning heater indoors or in a garage.
Do not taste food to decide whether it is safe.

title: Conscious adult or child choking
steps: If the person can cough or speak, encourage coughing and watch closely.
If they cannot cough, speak, or breathe, have someone contact emergency services if reachable.
Give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades.
Give 5 abdominal thrusts for a non-pregnant adult or child over 1 year. Use chest thrusts for pregnancy or when you cannot encircle the abdomen.
Repeat sets of 5 back blows and 5 thrusts until the object clears or the person becomes unresponsive.
avoid: Do not perform a blind finger sweep.
Do not use abdominal thrusts on an infant under 1 year.

title: Thermal burn
steps: Stop contact with the heat source and make sure the scene is safe.
Cool the burn under clean, cool running water for 20 minutes when available.
Remove jewelry and loose clothing near the burn unless stuck to skin.
Cover loosely with sterile non-stick dressing or clean plastic film.
Seek urgent medical care for deep, large, electrical, chemical, face, hand, genital, or airway burns.
avoid: Do not use ice, butter, grease, toothpaste, or adhesive dressings.
Do not break blisters or pull away material stuck to the burn.

title: Possible broken bone
steps: Control serious bleeding first and keep the person still.
Support the injured area in the position found using padding.
If help will be delayed and movement is necessary, splint above and below the injury without forcing it straight.
Check circulation beyond the injury before and after splinting.
Use a wrapped cold pack for swelling and seek medical evaluation.
avoid: Do not push an exposed bone back in or straighten a deformed limb.
Do not move a person with a possible spine, pelvis, or thigh injury unless immediate danger requires it.
source_name: American Red Cross - First Aid Steps

title: Shock after injury or illness
steps: Treat the cause you can see, especially severe bleeding.
Lay the person flat if breathing is comfortable and there is no suspected spine, pelvis, or leg injury.
Keep the person warm without overheating.
Reassure them, keep them still, and monitor breathing.
Seek emergency medical help by any available route.
avoid: Do not give food or drink.
Do not raise the legs if it causes pain, breathing difficulty, or you suspect a serious injury.

title: Seizure
steps: Clear nearby hard or sharp objects and cushion the person's head.
Time the seizure and loosen tight clothing around the neck.
When movement stops, place the person on their side if breathing.
Stay with them and speak calmly as awareness returns.
Seek emergency help for a seizure lasting 5 minutes or more, repeated seizures, injury, pregnancy, breathing trouble, or a first seizure.
avoid: Do not restrain the person.
Do not put anything in their mouth or give food, water, or medicine until fully alert.

title: Severe allergic reaction
steps: Use the person's epinephrine auto-injector immediately if severe symptoms are present and the device is available.
Contact emergency medical help by any available route.
Have the person lie down with legs raised if breathing allows; let them sit up if breathing is difficult.
A second prescribed dose may be needed after 5 to 15 minutes if symptoms persist and help has not arrived.
Monitor breathing and start CPR if the person becomes unresponsive and stops breathing normally.
avoid: Do not rely on antihistamines for airway swelling or breathing difficulty.
Do not let the person stand or walk suddenly.

title: Low blood sugar
steps: If the person is awake and can swallow, give 15 grams of fast-acting carbohydrate, such as glucose tablets or about 4 ounces of juice.
Wait 15 minutes and recheck blood sugar if a meter is available.
Repeat fast-acting carbohydrate if the level remains low or symptoms continue.
Once improved, give a snack containing carbohydrate and protein if the next meal is not soon.
If unconscious or unable to swallow, use prescribed glucagon if available and trained, place on their side, and seek emergency help.
avoid: Do not give food or drink to an unconscious or poorly responsive person.
Do not give insulin for suspected low blood sugar.

title: Possible poisoning
steps: Move away from fumes or spilled material only if you can do so safely.
Check breathing and start CPR if needed, using a barrier device if available.
Identify the substance, amount, and time of exposure. Keep the container or label.
Contact a poison center or emergency medical service by any available route.
Follow substance-specific professional instructions.
avoid: Do not make the person vomit unless a poison specialist directs it.
Do not give food, drink, charcoal, or a home remedy unless directed.

title: Chemical in the eye
steps: Protect yourself from the chemical and remove contaminated gloves or clothing.
Immediately rinse the eye with clean lukewarm water for at least 20 minutes.
Hold the eyelids open and let water flow away from the unaffected eye.
Remove contact lenses if they come out easily during rinsing.
Seek urgent medical evaluation and bring the product label if safe.
avoid: Do not rub the eye.
Do not try to neutralize one chemical with another or put drops in unless directed.

title: Earthquake
steps: Drop to your hands and knees, cover your head and neck, and hold on to sturdy shelter.
If indoors, stay inside and away from windows. If outdoors, move to an open area away from buildings and wires.
After shaking stops, expect aftershocks and check for injuries and fire.
Leave a damaged building by stairs when the route is safe.
If near the coast and shaking was strong or long, move to high ground for possible tsunami.
avoid: Do not run outside during shaking.
Do not use elevators or enter visibly damaged buildings.

title: Tornado
steps: Go to a basement, storm shelter, or small interior room on the lowest floor.
Stay away from windows, doors, and outside walls.
Protect your head and neck with your arms, a helmet, mattress, or heavy blanket.
In a vehicle, reach a sturdy shelter if it is close and safe.
Keep listening for warnings and wait for an official all-clear when available.
avoid: Do not shelter under a highway overpass.
Do not try to outrun a tornado through congested or uncertain roads.

title: Hurricane or severe coastal storm
steps: Evacuate promptly when authorities order it, especially from storm-surge zones.
If sheltering, use a small interior room away from windows on the lowest level not threatened by flooding.
Store safe water, medication, lights, batteries, and a radio before conditions worsen.
Keep devices charged and turn refrigerators colder before power loss.
Remain sheltered through the eye of the storm; dangerous winds return from the opposite direction.
avoid: Do not ignore evacuation orders.
Do not enter floodwater or use fuel-burning equipment indoors.

title: Wildfire
steps: Leave early when told to evacuate; use the planned route and a backup route.
Close windows and doors and turn off ventilation that draws outdoor smoke inside.
Wear an N95 respirator if available when exposed to smoke.
If trapped, call or signal your location, move to a cleared area with little fuel, and cover exposed skin.
Afterward, watch for hot spots, damaged trees, and downed utility lines.
avoid: Do not wait until flames are visible before evacuating.
Do not use a wet cloth as a substitute for a respirator against fine smoke particles.

title: Carbon monoxide exposure
steps: Move everyone into fresh air immediately without putting yourself at risk.
Contact emergency services and the fire department by any available route.
Check breathing and start CPR if needed.
Keep people away from the building or vehicle until it has been declared safe.
Seek medical evaluation even if symptoms improve.
avoid: Do not re-enter to find the source.
Never run a generator, grill, vehicle, or other fuel-burning device in a home, garage, or near openings.

title: Cleaning a minor wound
steps: Wash or sanitize your hands and use gloves if available.
Control bleeding with gentle direct pressure.
Rinse the wound with clean running water and remove loose visible dirt with clean tweezers.
Apply a thin layer of petroleum jelly or suitable antibiotic ointment if available and tolerated.
Cover with a sterile dressing and replace it when wet or dirty. Watch for spreading redness, warmth, swelling, pus, fever, or worsening pain.
avoid: Do not pour bleach or harsh disinfectant into a wound.
Do not close a deep, dirty, puncture, animal-bite, or heavily contaminated wound yourself.

title: Food safety during an outage
steps: Keep refrigerator and freezer doors closed as much as possible.
Use refrigerated perishable food first, then freezer food, then shelf-stable food.
Discard refrigerated perishables after 4 hours without a cold source.
Discard food that contacted floodwater or unsafe water.
When in doubt, throw it out.
avoid: Do not taste food to test safety.
Do not rely on smell or appearance alone.
source_name: CDC - Keep Food Safe After an Emergency

title: Emergency sanitation
steps: Keep human waste away from living, cooking, and water-storage areas.
If toilets cannot flush, line a sturdy bucket with two heavy bags and add absorbent material after each use.
Seal waste bags and store them away from people, animals, and water until local disposal is available.
Wash hands with safe water and soap after toilet use and before food handling.
Disinfect frequently touched surfaces with an appropriate labeled disinfectant.
avoid: Do not empty waste near wells, streams, gardens, or floodwater.
Do not mix bleach with ammonia, acids, or other cleaners.

title: Lost without GPS
steps: Stop moving, take slow breaths, and assess immediate hazards, daylight, weather, water, and injuries.
Stay near the last known route unless the location is dangerous.
Make yourself visible with bright material, a signal mirror, whistle, or three repeated signals.
Conserve phone battery by using airplane mode and checking for signal at planned intervals.
Move only with a clear reason, direction, and ability to return; leave markers and a written note.
avoid: Do not wander downhill or follow an unknown stream automatically.
Do not split the group unless immediate safety requires it.
source_name: US Forest Service - If You Get Lost

title: Emergency signaling
steps: Choose an open, safe location visible from the air or likely travel routes.
Use three repeated whistle blasts, flashes, fires, or other signals, then pause and repeat.
Make a large ground symbol with contrasting material.
Use a mirror or reflective object to sweep the horizon and aircraft.
Keep one signal method ready at night and another for daylight.
avoid: Do not create a signal fire when wildfire risk is high or fire cannot be contained.
Do not exhaust batteries with continuous transmission when scheduled checks will work.
source_name: US Forest Service - If You Get Lost

title: Emergency shelter in cold conditions
steps: Choose ground away from falling branches, flood channels, avalanche paths, and exposed ridges.
Block wind first, then insulate yourself from the ground with dry material.
Keep the shelter small enough to retain body heat but ventilated.
Change out of wet clothing and cover the head and neck.
Use shared body heat carefully while keeping clothing dry.
avoid: Do not sleep directly on cold ground.
Do not use a flame or fuel-burning heater in an unventilated shelter.

title: Lightning
steps: Move into a substantial building or hard-topped metal vehicle.
Inside, stay away from windows, plumbing, corded devices, and concrete walls or floors.
If no shelter exists, move away from isolated trees, ridges, water, fences, and metal objects.
Spread group members apart to reduce multiple casualties.
Wait at least 30 minutes after the last thunder before leaving shelter.
avoid: Do not lie flat on the ground.
Small open shelters, tents, and covered porches do not protect from lightning.

title: Frostbite
steps: Move to shelter and remove wet clothing and constricting jewelry.
Protect the area from pressure and refreezing.
If refreezing can be prevented, warm the area in circulating water around 99 to 104 F (37 to 40 C).
Separate affected fingers or toes with clean dry material and elevate if possible.
Seek medical care as soon as possible.
avoid: Do not rub, massage, walk on frostbitten feet, or use direct dry heat.
Do not thaw tissue if it may freeze again.

title: Asthma attack
steps: Sit the person upright, keep them calm, and move away from smoke or known triggers.
Help them use their prescribed quick-relief inhaler with a spacer if available.
Follow their written asthma action plan.
Seek emergency help if they cannot speak in full sentences, lips look blue or gray, symptoms worsen, or the inhaler gives little relief.
Monitor breathing and be ready to start CPR if they become unresponsive.
avoid: Do not make the person lie flat.
Do not use someone else's prescription medicine.
